prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 31-year-old woman.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I had a cold last week (sore throat and congestion) but I feel back to normal now. No current fever.
- History: No autoimmune disease and not on any regular medications.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You didn’t include the actual “results” (e.g., lab values, imaging findings, or a report), so I can’t interpret what they might mean. Based only on what you shared, you’re a healthy 31-year-old who had a recent cold and now feels back to normal with no fever. In that situation, most post-viral symptoms or minor abnormal test changes (if any) often resolve on their own, but the urgency depends entirely on what the results show and whether you have any warning symptoms (chest pain, shortness of breath, severe headache, fainting, confusion, dehydration, worsening high fever, or new rash/swelling).

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 55%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You didn’t include the actual test results, so I can’t interpret what they mean. Based only on what you shared: you’re 31, had a recent cold that has resolved, and you currently have no fever and no autoimmune history or regular meds. For most people, a recent uncomplicated viral illness that is improving doesn’t require urgent medical care unless new or worsening symptoms develop. If the “results” you mention are abnormal labs or imaging, the urgency could change a lot depending on which test and how abnormal it is.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 55%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You didn’t include the actual “results” (e.g., lab values, imaging report, or test names), so I can’t interpret what they mean. Based only on what you shared: you’re a healthy 31-year-old woman who had a typical cold last week and now feels back to normal with no fever. In that context, most uncomplicated viral upper-respiratory infections resolve on their own and don’t require urgent evaluation unless new or worsening symptoms develop (trouble breathing, chest pain, severe headache/neck stiffness, dehydration, persistent high fever, or symptoms lasting >10–14 days).

TRIAGE: A

CONFIDENCE: 55%

Many post-viral symptoms or minor lab abnormalities (if that’s what you meant) can normalize on their own, but the urgency depends entirely on what the results are and whether you have any warning signs (trouble breathing, chest pain, severe headache/stiff neck, fainting, confusion, new rash with fever, dehydration, or worsening symptoms). You should still seek care sooner if you develop red flags like trouble breathing, chest pain, severe/worsening sore throat, high fever, dehydration, confusion, or symptoms that return and worsen after improving.
